response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, I think dealing with fake content on social media is something everyone struggles with these days, even though I'm not on there all that much myself. Working as a waiter in Philly, I hear people talking about crazy stuff they saw online all the time, and half the time it turns out to be completely made up. The thing is, I don't really have time to fact-check everything I see when I do scroll through my feeds a few times a month. Between work shifts and spending time with my wife, social media isn't really my priority anyway. I'd rather be reading a good novel or playing videogames when I have downtime. But when I do come across something that seems off, I try to use common sense about it. Like, does this story sound too wild to be true? Is it trying to make me really angry or scared? Those are usually red flags. I think the best approach is just not taking everything at face value. My dad has his PhD and he always taught me to question things, even though school wasn't really my strong suit. I dropped out pretty early but I still picked up that lesson. When something seems sketchy, I'll sometimes ask my wife what she thinks or mention it to coworkers during our breaks at the restaurant. Getting other perspectives helps a lot. I also think it's important not to share stuff immediately just because it confirms what you already believe. That's hard for people because we all want to be right, but spreading fake news just makes the problem worse for everyone else. My Hindu beliefs teach me about truth and dharma, doing the right thing, so I try to apply that even online. I guess my advice would be to slow down, think twice before sharing, talk to people you trust in real life, and remember that not everything online deserves your attention or energy. Since I'm barely on social media anyway, it's easier for me to just disconnect when things get too messy or confusing. Sometimes the best way to deal with fake content is simply spending less time where it lives and more time in the real world with real people.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 22-year-old waiter with lower secondary education who spends minimal time on social media (few times monthly). His high agreeableness suggests a cooperative, trusting, and considerate tone. Low conscientiousness indicates a more relaxed, less systematic approach. His creative trait and hobbies (reading, videogames) suggest he values imagination but also escapism. His father's PhD education might have influenced his thinking despite his own limited formal education. Hindu beliefs provide moral framework. The moderate extraversion means he's social but not overly so. His occupation as a waiter in Philadelphia provides real-world grounding and exposure to diverse conversations. Being married at 22 suggests maturity in some areas. The tone should be casual, honest, relatable, non-preachy, and emphasize practical common-sense approaches over technical solutions. He wouldn't be overly systematic or detail-oriented given low conscientiousness, but would emphasize interpersonal checking and intuition over formal fact-checking methods.
